title: NIS2 Art.21(2)(b) — Incident Handling Playbook Adherence
industry: All sectors in NIS2 Annex I and II
exclusions: Does not assess whether the mandated playbook itself adequately addresses Art.21(2)(b) — that's a design decision for the IR lead, governed by the playbook-review cadence. Does not evaluate whether the IR team's manual interventions were correct. Does not measure response quality (only execution presence and completeness).
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.2.23` row-per-finding output, `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:saved_search` archive (7-year retention, RFC 3161 TSA-signed), `NIS2 Art.21(2)(b) — Playbook adherence` panel, per-incident playbook traces archived to `evidence:soar_playbook_run`.

compliance.assurance_rationale: The UC produces evidence that the documented incident-handling playbook was actually executed for each significant incident, with each step's start/end time captured. Demonstrating that the playbook itself adequately addresses Art.21(2)(b) is a process-design question for the IR lead — Splunk attests to execution but not to design adequacy.
compliance.controlObjective: Proves that for each significant incident, the mandated SOAR playbook for the relevant urgency tier ran to completion (or to a documented decision point), and that no playbook steps were silently skipped.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search `UC-22.2.23` row-per-finding output, `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:saved_search` archive, dashboard panel `NIS2 Art.21(2)(b) — Playbook adherence`. Per-incident playbook execution traces archived to `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:soar_playbook_run`.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Fixture `sample-data/uc-22.2.23-fixture.json` injects a high-urgency notable with `nis2_significant="yes"` and `urgency="critical"` mapped to mandated_playbook `"nis2_critical_response"` in `nis2_playbook_mandate.csv`. The corresponding SOAR container has playbook_run with executed_playbook=`"basic_triage"` (different from mandated). The search returns `finding="WRONG_PLAYBOOK"`.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Same fixture but the SOAR container has playbook_run with executed_playbook=`"nis2_critical_response"`, status=`"completed"`, steps_succeeded=`mandated_steps_count`. The search returns 0 rows.

dataSources: ES `notable` macro for high/critical urgency notables; `index=phantom sourcetype=phantom:container label=nis2_significant_incident` for SOAR cases; `index=phantom sourcetype=phantom:playbook_run` for playbook execution records; `index=phantom sourcetype=phantom:action_run` for individual SOAR action results. Lookup `nis2_playbook_mandate.csv` mapping (urgency, nis2_tier) to mandated playbook name; `nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv`.
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263) ≥7.0; Splunk Add-on for Phantom (Splunkbase 3411) ≥3.0; Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621) ≥5.3.
spl:
`notable` urgency IN ("high","critical") earliest=-3d
| lookup nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv rule_name OUTPUT nis2_tier nis2_significant
| where coalesce(nis2_significant, "unclassified") != "no"
| lookup nis2_playbook_mandate.csv urgency nis2_tier OUTPUT mandated_playbook mandated_steps_count
| join type=left event_id [
    search index=phantom sourcetype=phantom:container label=nis2_significant_incident earliest=-3d
    | rename source_data_identifier as event_id
    | stats latest(container_id) as container_id, latest(_time) as soar_open_time by event_id
  ]
| join type=left container_id [
    search index=phantom sourcetype=phantom:playbook_run earliest=-3d
    | stats latest(playbook_name) as executed_playbook, latest(status) as playbook_status, latest(_time) as playbook_completed_time, count(eval(action_status="success")) as steps_succeeded by container_id
  ]
| eval playbook_status = coalesce(playbook_status, "NOT_STARTED")
| eval playbook_match = if(executed_playbook = mandated_playbook, "MATCH", "MISMATCH")
| eval steps_completion_pct = round(100 * coalesce(steps_succeeded, 0) / mandated_steps_count, 1)
| eval finding = case(
    playbook_status = "NOT_STARTED", "NO_PLAYBOOK",
    playbook_match = "MISMATCH", "WRONG_PLAYBOOK",
    playbook_status = "running" AND (now() - soar_open_time) > 7200, "STALLED",
    steps_completion_pct < 80 AND playbook_status = "completed", "INCOMPLETE",
    1==1, "OK")
| where finding != "OK"
| eval evidence_id = sha256(rule_name . event_id . _time . "playbook")
| table _time, rule_name, urgency, nis2_tier, owner, mandated_playbook, executed_playbook, playbook_status, steps_completion_pct, finding, evidence_id
| sort - _time
description: Detects significant incidents where the mandated SOAR playbook for the urgency tier did not start, an unexpected playbook ran instead, the playbook stalled (>2h running), or completed with <80% of mandated steps succeeding. Each finding is annotated with the specific failure mode.
value: Auditors challenge 'we have an IR playbook' with 'show me where it actually ran for the last incident'. Without execution evidence, the operator's Art.21(2)(b) IR claim collapses. This UC ties each significant notable to the SOAR playbook that should have run and surfaces every gap before the next audit cycle.
implementation: (1) Build and maintain `nis2_playbook_mandate.csv` with one row per (urgency, nis2_tier) tuple naming the mandated playbook. (2) Schedule every 1 hour. (3) PagerDuty page on `finding=NO_PLAYBOOK`; email digest on `WRONG_PLAYBOOK`/`STALLED`/`INCOMPLETE`. (4) Throttle by `event_id` for 4 hours.
visualization: Single value: incidents without correct playbook. Pie chart: finding type breakdown. Sortable table with deep-link drilldown to SOAR. Trellis: 30-day playbook-adherence trend.
cimSpl:
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Alerts.Alerts where Alerts.severity IN ("high","critical") AND _time >= relative_time(now(), "-3d") by Alerts.signature, Alerts._time, Alerts.signature_id
| rename Alerts.* as *
dataModelAcceleration: Enable `Alerts` acceleration; `Change` acceleration if you want to extend to non-incident SOAR playbooks.

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Phantom on Splunkbase
ENISA NIS2 Technical Implementation Guidance — IR processes

knownFalsePositives: 1) **Mandated playbook updated mid-incident** — the IR team renamed or replaced the playbook, and the existing run is against the old name. Distinguish: `nis2_playbook_mandate.csv` `last_modified` is recent AND the executed_playbook name is the previous version. Suppress: maintain a `playbook_alias.csv` mapping deprecated names to current ones; lookup the alias before the match. 2) **Manual override by IR lead** — the lead deliberately ran a different playbook because the situation required it (e.g. high-severity incident running a lower-tier playbook because the data isn't ingested for the higher-tier). Distinguish: SOAR case has an `override_reason` field populated. Suppress: time-bound exception in `nis2_exception_register.csv` keyed on `event_id` + `executed_playbook`. 3) **Playbook is a 'parent' that calls 'child' playbooks** — the parent's `playbook_run` shows complete; the child's individual runs show partial. Distinguish: `playbook_metadata.parent_playbook_run_id` is non-null. Suppress: in the SPL, dedupe by parent playbook run; sum child action counts into the parent. 4) **SOAR action timeout for an external integration** (e.g. ServiceNow API down) leaves an action in 'pending' which is counted as not-succeeded. Distinguish: `action_run | search status=pending action=create_ticket | stats count by container_id`. Suppress: in the SPL, treat `pending` separately from `failed`; only `failed` counts as a step gap. 5) **Re-run after code change** — analyst re-ran the playbook to test a fix; the new run replaces the old run's status. Distinguish: `playbook_run | stats count by container_id, playbook_name | where count > 1`. Suppress: take `latest(_time) by container_id, playbook_name` (already in SPL). 6) **Containers stuck in 'New' because auto-assign disabled during maintenance** — the playbook never ran, but for an operational reason. Distinguish: SOAR auto-assign playbook last_run_time is older than the container's create_time. Suppress: time-bound exception during the maintenance window.
equipmentModels: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928, incident response task tracking)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, correlation searches and notable events)
Splunk SOAR 6.x (Splunkbase 3411, playbook execution and task automation)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `phantom` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv')`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.21(2)(b)`. The control objective is: "Proves that for each significant incident, the mandated SOAR playbook for the relevant urgency tier ran to completion (or to a documented decision point), and that no playbook steps were silently skipped." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `phantom` under `phantom:container`, `phantom:playbook_run`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Build and maintain `nis2_playbook_mandate.csv` with one row per (urgency, nis2_tier) tuple naming the mandated playbook. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 2:** Schedule every 1 hour. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** PagerDuty page on `finding=NO_PLAYBOOK`; email digest on `WRONG_PLAYBOOK`/`STALLED`/`INCOMPLETE`. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

• **Hint 4:** Throttle by `event_id` for 4 hours. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "NIS2 Art.21(2)(b) — Incident Handling Playbook Adherence," the aggregation summarises incident flow — volume per category, time-to-contain distribution, and resolution cadence are the metrics regulators ask about during post-incident review.

This search correlates the outer dataset (index=`phantom`, sourcetype=`phantom:container`) with a subsearch on `event_id` across the last 3 days. `type=left` preserves all rows from the outer search; nulls in the inner side indicate missing data that may itself constitute a finding.

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Alerts` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Alerts.Alerts where Alerts.severity IN ("high","critical") AND _time >= relative_time(now(), "-3d") by Alerts.signature, Alerts._time, Alerts.signature_id
| rename Alerts.* as *
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Alerts` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.23: NIS2 Art.21(2)(b) — Incident Handling Playbook Adherence]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -3d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.23,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=phantom sourcetype=phantom:container earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `_time`, `rule_name`, `urgency`, `nis2_tier`, `owner`, `mandated_playbook`, `executed_playbook`, `playbook_status`, `steps_completion_pct`, `finding`. Run:

```spl
index=phantom sourcetype=phantom:container earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table _time, rule_name, urgency, nis2_tier, owner, mandated_playbook, executed_playbook, playbook_status
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `steps_completion_pct`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(steps_completion_pct) as p50 perc90(steps_completion_pct) as p90 perc99(steps_completion_pct) as p99 max(steps_completion_pct) as hi
```

The current threshold `80` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.23: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Single Value: Incidents Without Correct Playbook. Pie Chart: Finding Type Breakdown. Sortable Table With Deep-Link Drilldown To Soar. Trellis: 30-Day Playbook-Adherence Trend (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

• 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

• Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

• Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

• Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: We don't just have a written playbook for handling cyber incidents — we check that the right one actually ran for every serious incident. If a step was skipped or the wrong recipe was used, this watch tells us in time to fix it before the auditor asks.
